  • Replications
    • FDID — Forward Difference-in-Differences (Li 2024)
    • TSSC — Two-Step Synthetic Control (Li & Shankar 2024)
    • VanillaSC — Standard Synthetic Control (ADH 2010/2015; Abadie-Gardeazabal 2003)
    • Ridge ASCM — Augmented Synthetic Control (Ben-Michael, Feller & Rothstein 2021)
    • SparseSC — L1 Predictor Selection for Synthetic Controls (Vives-i-Bastida 2022)
    • NSC — Nonlinear Synthetic Control (Tian 2023)
    • SDID — Synthetic Difference-in-Differences (Arkhangelsky et al. 2021)
    • MCNNM — Matrix Completion with Nuclear-Norm Minimization (Athey et al. 2021)
    • SpSyDiD — Spatial Synthetic-DiD (Serenini & Masek 2024)
    • SEQ_SDID — Sequential Synthetic DiD (Arkhangelsky & Samkov 2025)
    • ClusterSC — Synthetic Control with Donor Selection (Rho et al. 2025)
    • LEXSCM — Synthetic Experimental Design (Abadie-Zhao 2026; Vives-i-Bastida 2022)
    • SCMO — Synthetic Control with Multiple Outcomes (Tian et al. 2026; Sun et al. 2025)
    • RESCM — SCM-relaxation (Liao, Shi & Zheng 2026)
    • LINF / L1LINF — L-infinity-norm SC (Wang, Xing & Ye 2025)
    • GEOLIFT — Meta’s GeoLift walkthrough (augsynth cross-validation)
    • ROLLDID — Lee & Wooldridge rolling-transformation DiD
    • PPSCM — augsynth multisynth (Paglayan collective bargaining)
    • SNN — deshen24/syntheticNN (Prop 99)
    • SYNDES — Doudchenko et al. (2021) BLS Monte Carlo
    • SPCD — Lu et al. (2022) Prop 99 design study
    • SPOTSYNTH — O’Riordan & Gilligan-Lee (2025) spillover detection
    • MAREX — Abadie & Zhao (2026) Walmart design (independent validator)
    • DSC — Distributional Synthetic Controls on Dube (2019)
    • MicroSynth — panel method vs the R microsynth (Seattle DMI)
  • Benchmarks
  • References

Observational: canonical workhorses

  • Forward Difference-in-Differences (FDID)

  • Synthetic Business Cycle (SBC)
  • Harmonic Synthetic Control (HSC)

Observational: generalised estimand / treatment / unit

  • Synthetic Control with Multiple Outcomes (SCMO)
  • Continuous-Treatment Synthetic Control (CTSC)
  • Distributional Synthetic Control (DSC)
  • Synthetic Interventions (SI)
  • MicroSynth (User-Level Balancing SC)

Observational: convex-hull relaxation

  • Imperfect Synthetic Controls (ISCM)
  • Nonlinear Synthetic Control (NSC)

Observational: No Donors

  • Synthetic Historical Control (SHC)

Observational: high-dimensional donors

  • Bayesian Synthetic Control with a Soft Simplex Constraint (BVS-SS)
  • Cluster Synthetic Controls (CLUSTERSC)
  • Multi-Level Synthetic Control (mlSC)
  • Forward-Selected Synthetic Control (FSCM)
  • Matching and Synthetic Control (MASC)
  • Multivariate Square-root Lasso Synthetic Control (MSQRT)
  • Sparse Synthetic Control (SparseSC)
  • Panel Data Approach (PDA)
  • Relaxed / Penalized Synthetic Control (RESCM)

Observational: time-aware and factor models

  • Time-Aware Synthetic Control (TASC)
  • Factor Model Approach (FMA)
  • Dynamic Synthetic Control for Auto-Regressive processes (DSCAR)

Observational: staggered adoption

  • Synthetic Difference-in-Differences (SDID)
  • Sequential Synthetic Difference-in-Differences (Sequential SDiD)
  • Partially Pooled SCM (PPSCM)
  • Staggered Synthetic Control (SSC)

Observational: spillover-aware

  • Spatial Synthetic Difference-in-Differences (SpSyDiD)
  • Spillover-Aware Synthetic Control (SPILLSYNTH)
  • Spillover-Detecting Synthetic Control (SPOTSYNTH)

Observational: missing data

  • Matrix Completion with Nuclear Norm Minimization (MCNNM)
  • Synthetic Nearest Neighbors / Causal Matrix Completion (SNN)
  • Robust Matrix estimation with Side Information (RMSI)

Observational: endogenous treatment

  • Synthetic IV
  • Proximal Inference Synthetic Control (PROXIMAL)

Experimental design

  • Synthetic Controls for Experimental Design (MAREX)
  • Synthetic Design (SYNDES)
  • Parallel-Trends Supergeo Design (PANGEO)
  • Synthetic Principal Component Design (SPCD)
  • Modified Unbiased Synthetic Control
  • Rolling-Transformation DiD (ROLLDID)

MicroSynth — panel method vs the R microsynth (Seattle DMI)

Contents

  • What the panel method does
  • Data
  • Result
  • JSS Table 2 (multi-outcome joint match)
  • Reproduce

MicroSynth — panel method vs the R microsynth (Seattle DMI)#

Cross-validation of MicroSynth’s panel method (weight_method="panel") against the R microsynth package (Robbins, Saunders & Kilmer 2017, JASA; Robbins & Davenport 2021, JSS v97i02) on the package’s canonical Seattle Drug Market Intervention example.

What the panel method does#

Reading microsynth/R/weights.r: when match.out (lagged outcomes) is supplied, the package’s my.qp (a LowRankQP solve) chooses control weights by a non-negative quadratic program — exactly balance the covariate totals (an intercept makes the weights sum to the treated count) and least-squares-fit the lagged outcomes, with \(w \\ge 0\). Raking (survey::calibrate) is only the covariate-only initialization/fallback, not the panel weights themselves.

That objective is rank-deficient over a large control pool: it constrains the weights only through a handful of totals (here 10 covariate + 12 lagged-outcome constraints across ~9600 controls), so the counterfactual is not identified by the constraints alone. On this data the feasible period-13 effect ranges over roughly \([-392, +153]\); LowRankQP simply returns its interior-point iterate. mlsynth adds a strictly-convex ridge (panel_ridge) that selects the unique minimum-norm / maximum-ESS optimum — the most diffuse synthetic control consistent with exact covariate balance and the best lagged-outcome fit. Because LowRankQP’s interior-point solution is itself near that point, the two coincide to 3–4 significant figures, making this a genuine cross-validation rather than a comparison of solver artifacts.

Data#

basedata/seattledmi.parquet — the R microsynth package’s seattledmi dataset (data(seattledmi)), trimmed to the columns this case uses (ID/time/Intervention + the four Table 2 outcomes + the 9 census covariates). Full panel: 9642 census blocks × 16 periods, 39 treated blocks, Intervention on from time >= 13.

Configuration matches mlsynth’s one-outcome MicroSynth exactly: match.out = c("any_crime"), match.covar = the 9 census covariates, start.pre = 1, end.pre = 12, end.post = 16.

Result#

Per-period total treatment effect on any_crime (Treated − synthetic Control):

Period

mlsynth

R microsynth

13

−33.06

−33.06

14

−74.43

−74.35

15

−45.35

−45.45

16

−64.86

−64.89

ATT

−54.43

−54.44

Per-period effects agree to ~0.1 crimes and the ATT to ~0.01. The identified quantities both packages pin agree exactly: weights sum to the treated count (39) and covariate + lagged-outcome balance is exact (max |SMD| ≈ 1e-10). The ridge-selected optimum has effective sample size ≈ 378 (the most diffuse control consistent with the fit).

The placebo-permutation test (mlsynth run_inference=True, permutation_test="lower") finds the crime reduction significant: the observed ATT lies well below every placebo group (≈ 7 placebo SDs out), so the one-sided p-value sits at the 1/(1 + n_permutations) floor — matching the R package’s reported significant reductions on this example.

JSS Table 2 (multi-outcome joint match)#

The package’s headline analysis (sea1 in the JSS vignette) matches all four outcomes jointly — one synthetic control balancing every outcome’s pre-period trajectory plus the covariates — and reports a cumulative effect per outcome (Table 2, top panel). mlsynth reproduces it with match_outcomes set to the four outcomes (run once per primary outcome; the shared weight vector is identical across runs). Cumulative Pct.Chng over the post window 13–16:

Outcome

mlsynth

R microsynth

i_felony

−32.6%

−32.6%

i_misdemea

−37.3%

−37.3%

i_drugs

−15.9%

−15.8%

any_crime

−20.1%

−20.1%

All four match to <0.5 percentage points (Trt/Con levels agree to ~0.1 crimes). As in the paper, the permutation test flags felonies, misdemeanors and total crime as significant reductions but not drug crimes — the JSS Perm.pVal column (felony 0.020, misdemea 0.008, drugs 0.304, any_crime 0.020) under test="lower".

Note

What is and isn’t reproduced. The top panel’s point estimates (Trt/Con/Pct.Chng) are reproduced exactly. The top panel’s Linear p-values/CIs are a survey Taylor-linearization variance estimate that mlsynth does not implement; mlsynth’s inference is the placebo permutation, so it is the package’s Perm column that is the comparison target.

Note

R reference is baked in, not run in CI. microsynth does not install from CRAN in the CI/sandbox network (CRAN-over-HTTPS is firewalled). The reference numbers above come from benchmarks/R/microsynth_seattle.R (R microsynth 2.0.51), which documents the apt + GitHub-mirror install route and regenerates them. The benchmark asserts mlsynth’s output matches the baked reference within tolerance.

Reproduce#

python benchmarks/run_benchmarks.py --case microsynth_seattle
# regenerate the R references (needs R microsynth installed):
Rscript benchmarks/R/microsynth_seattle.R    # single-outcome any_crime
Rscript benchmarks/R/microsynth_table2.R     # JSS Table 2 joint match
